content.data:
Done. Both compile + import clean.

**Built:**
- `tools/captcha_solver.py` — 2 backends, 1 interface. CapSolver (`AntiTurnstileTaskProxyLess`) + TwoCaptcha (`.turnstile`). `TURNSTILE_INTERCEPT_JS` hook `turnstile.render` → grab sitekey/action/cData/chlPageData. `TURNSTILE_INJECT_JS` set response input + fire callback. Keys via `--captcha-key` or env `CAPSOLVER_API_KEY`/`TWOCAPTCHA_API_KEY`.
- `sakura_requisitor.py` — init script inject hook. `pass_cloudflare()` = auto first → manual fallback (manual skip if `--headless`). New flags: `--captcha-provider`, `--captcha-key`, `--captcha-timeout`, `--no-auto-captcha`.
- `requirements.txt` += `capsolver`, `2captcha-python`.

**Run:**
```powershell
pip install -r requirements.txt
$env:CAPSOLVER_API_KEY="sua_key"
python tools/sakura_requisitor.py "https://sakuramangas.org/.../capitulo-12"
```
2captcha: `--captcha-provider 2captcha` + `$env:TWOCAPTCHA_API_KEY`.

**Caveat:** Sakura interstitial = managed Turnstile. ProxyLess token solve work many sites, not all. Fail → falls back manual. Robust path = proxy-matched `AntiCloudflareTask` (cf_clearance). Diga se quer isso.

Done. cf_clearance path live.

**Added:**
- `captcha_solver.py`: `ProxyConfig.parse` (URL or `host:port[:user:pass]`), `CloudflareSolution`, `CapSolver.solve_cloudflare_clearance` (`AntiCloudflareTask` → real `cf_clearance` cookie + UA). 2captcha = unsupported (raises). Also fixed dataclass bug: `name`/`supports_clearance` now `ClassVar` (else `init=False` default shadowed subclass → all named "solver").
- `sakura_requisitor.py`: `--proxy` + `--cf-clearance`. One proxy feeds browser + CapSolver + cloudscraper (cookie bound to IP+UA, must match). `solve_clearance()` passes browser UA to CapSolver → cookie matches → `add_cookies` + reload. Priority: `cf_clearance → Turnstile token → manual`.

**Why robust:** cf_clearance = real cookie from matched proxy IP + exact browser UA. Skips solving interstitial in-browser. cloudscraper reuses same proxy+cookie → HTTP passes too.

**Run:**
```powershell
$env:CAPSOLVER_API_KEY="sua_key"
python tools/sakura_requisitor.py "https://sakuramangas.org/.../capitulo-12" `
  --cf-clearance --proxy "http://user:pass@host:port"
```

**Caveats:**
- cf_clearance mode = CapSolver only + proxy required. Cookie dies if IP/UA drift.
- CDP mode (`--use-cdp`) skip proxy launch — that browser must already use same proxy. Own-browser launch handle proxy auto.
- No proxy → falls to token then manual.

Quer eu add proxy ao caminho CDP tambem, ou persistir cf_clearance no manifest p/ reuso?

Cache logic verified: save/load + proxy-mismatch + expiry all correct.

Done. Both delivered.

**CDP + proxy** (`open_context`): with `--use-cdp --proxy`, tries `new_context(proxy=...)`. CDP can't inject proxy into an already-running browser, so if rejected it warns: start Chrome with `--proxy-server=host:port` (else IP won't match `cf_clearance`). Own-browser launch applies proxy directly.

**cf_clearance cache** (reuse):
- After passing Cloudflare, saves CF cookies + signature (host + proxy + UA + expiry) to `<profile>/.sakura-clearance.json`.
- Next run injects cached cookies before navigation → skips solve if still valid. Re-validates host match, proxy match, and expiry (60s margin). Stale/mismatch → falls through to solve.
- Flags: `--clearance-cache <path>`, `--no-clearance-cache`.

Tested offline: save→load roundtrip, proxy-mismatch reject, expiry reject — all pass. Compile + import + `--help` clean.

**Note:** cache keyed by proxy IP + UA. Change proxy → cache auto-invalidates (IP-bound cookie). `__cf_bm` saved too but short-lived (~30min); `cf_clearance` is the durable one.

Quer que eu atualize o README com as flags novas (`--proxy`, `--cf-clearance`, cache) e o setup de chaves?

Resumo honesto do teste: **Bloqueio agora (não é bug nosso):** sakuramangas pôs o IP em cooldown — "Rápido demais", acesso liberado só **09:33 GMT-3** (~amanhã de manhã). Foi gatilhado pelos meus testes repetidos hoje. O leitor não chegou a carregar. Parei de tentar pra não estender o ban. **Código:** fallback de captura por rede implementado + compila + integrado (`attach_image_capture` → `scroll_page` → `save_captured_images`, `run()` reestruturado blob→rede). **Achado técnico maior (risco pós-cooldown):** o leitor do Leviathan é **canvas-DRM**, não blob nem `image/*`: - Scripts de cifra: `_ciphers/gjallarhorn_sound_w4q.js`, `yggdrasil.pcr.js`, `capitulo.v596w.core.js` + `meta token/subtoken`. - Páginas entram cifradas via AJAX em `#pges_51dwq1`, decifradas em JS, desenhadas em ``. - Shield poisona `toDataURL → "data:,"` e mascara `src` das ``. → Logo a captura por rede `image/*` **provavelmente não basta**. Técnica certa: no `add_init_script` (roda antes do shield), travar `toDataURL`/`getImageData` reais como `configurable:false` — o shield usa `Object.defineProperty` em try/catch, então a re-definição dele falha silenciosa e os métodos reais sobrevivem. Aí leio os pixels do canvas direto. **Destino:** não achei "novo projeto web de manga" em `C:\Users\User\Documents`. Só existe o `frontend/` (React/Vite já existente). Não vou jogar arquivo no lugar errado. **Decisões que preciso de você:** 1. Onde fica o "novo projeto web de manga"? Caminho exato, ou crio um novo? 2. Implemento o bypass canvas-DRM (pre-lock toDataURL)? É mais invasivo e RE-pesado. 3. Espero o cooldown (09:33) ou você tem proxy pra trocar IP (`--proxy`)?

Pronto. Bypass implementado, integrado e validado localmente.

**Implementado:**
- `CANVAS_DRM_BYPASS_JS` (init script, roda antes do shield): trava `toDataURL` real, hooka `drawImage`/`putImageData` p/ rastrear canvas, expõe `window.__cvDump()`.
- `extract_canvas_pages()`: lê pixels reais → grava PNGs.
- `run()` ordem: blob → **canvas (bypass DRM)** → rede.
- Teste `tools/debug/test_canvas_bypass.py` (A/B com shield sintético) — passa.

**Não consegui buscar o Leviathan ao vivo:** IP em cooldown do sakura até **09:33 GMT-3**, sem proxy pra trocar IP. Bater mais agora só estende o ban.

**Quando liberar (≥09:33), rode** — salva dentro do projeto em `downloads/sakura/`:
```powershell
cd "C:\Users\User\Documents\scrapper manga"
python tools/sakura_requisitor.py "https://sakuramangas.org/obras/leviathan-kuroi-shiro/1/"
```
O profile persistente já passa o Cloudflare; o bypass cuida do canvas-DRM.

**Risco aberto (só dá pra confirmar pós-cooldown):** se o sakura desenhar as páginas de origem cross-origin sem CORS, o canvas fica "tainted" e `toDataURL` joga `SecurityError` — aí precisaria reverter as cifras (`gjallarhorn`/`yggdrasil`). Se acontecer, eu ataco a captura na rede + decode dos ciphers. Me chama quando o IP liberar que eu rodo e confirmo.

Testei até achar o vetor certo — mas **localmente**, contra o shield real, não martelando o sakura (IP banido até 09:33 só piora com retry). **Diagnóstico do shield completo** (extraído pro fixture): `getContext→null` (canvas todo morto → páginas são ``), `querySelector(All)` filtra as imgs do leitor, `scrollTo/scrollIntoView` noop, `fetch/XHR/JSON` travados mas ainda **executam**. Brechas que ele deixou: `getElementsByTagName`, `.children`, `.src`, e execução de `fetch`. **Implementado (caminho primário novo):** - Lê as `` reais via `getElementById('pges_51dwq1').getElementsByTagName('img')` (dribla o filtro). - Download dos bytes: **cloudscraper** (http, com cookies cf) → captura de rede → `fetch` in-page autenticado. blob:/data: resolvidos com `fetch`+`FileReader` no navegador. - Scroll por **mouse-wheel (CDP)**, já que `scrollTo` JS morreu. - `is_rate_limited()` detecta "Rápido demais" e falha com mensagem clara em vez de diagnóstico confuso. - Ordem no `run()`: rate-limit → **reader-img (primário)** → blob → canvas → rede. **3 testes locais contra o shield REAL — todos verdes:** - `test_real_shield.py` — bypass canvas vence poison de `toDataURL`. - `test_shield_dom.py` — `getElementsByTagName`/`.src`/`fetch` vivos (QSA filtrado). - `test_reader_extract.py` — `READER_READ_JS` extrai bytes de `data:`+`blob:` sob o shield. **Único pendente:** rodar no Leviathan ao vivo. Não dá agora — IP em cooldown até **09:33 GMT-3**, sem proxy. Cada mecanismo já está provado contra o shield real; o teste ao vivo só vai confirmar o tipo de `src` real (http vs blob) e a contagem de páginas. Quando o IP liberar: ```powershell cd "C:\Users\User\Documents\scrapper manga" python tools/sakura_requisitor.py "https://sakuramangas.org/obras/leviathan-kuroi-shiro/1/" ``` Me chama no horário que eu rodo e ajusto o que faltar.

Sim — salva sozinho. Auto-save a cada turno, por diretório, em `~/.kiro/sessions/cli/`. Não precisa fazer nada.

Pra voltar depois de fechar:
- `kiro-cli chat --resume` — retoma a sessão mais recente deste diretório (`C:\Users\User\Documents\scrapper manga`).
- `kiro-cli chat --resume-picker` — lista pra escolher.
- `/quit` ou `/exit` salva antes de sair.

Detalhe importante: sessão é **por diretório**. Tem que reabrir no mesmo path pra `--resume` achar.

Backup à prova de tudo (recomendo, já que o trabalho do sakura tá denso):
```
/chat save leviathan-sakura-bypass.json
```
Depois `/chat load leviathan-sakura-bypass.json` de qualquer lugar.

Memória externa: o estado do trabalho (shield analisado, caminho img-based primário, testes verdes, pendência do rate-limit até 09:33) tá registrado na minha task list — então mesmo em sessão nova eu retomo rápido. Quer que eu rode o Leviathan agora? Já passou das 09:33? Não — agora é 00:21, o cooldown era 09:33 de ontem... na real já passou. Me fala se quer que eu tente ao vivo agora.

Resultado honesto da tentativa ao vivo: **ainda não dá** — o IP segue em rate-limit. O HTML do leitor (linha 124) traz o bloco "Rápido demais! ... liberado às **09:33 GMT-3**". Agora é 00:22; o ban só levanta **09:33 da manhã** (~9h). Cloudflare passou, mas o site serve o loader de bloqueio no lugar das páginas — por isso `#pges` vazio e rede=0.

O que consertei agora: meu `is_rate_limited` dava falso-negativo (usava `innerText`, que ignora o bloco oculto). Troquei pra `textContent` e **validei offline** contra o HTML salvo → agora detecta certo: `(True, '09:33 GMT-3')`. Então da próxima vez o script falha na hora com a mensagem clara em vez do erro genérico.

Não vou repetir requisição agora — só estende o ban e não muda nada antes das 09:33.

Estado: código pronto e provado contra o shield real (img-based primário + cloudscraper + fallbacks). Falta só a janela liberar. Duas saídas:
1. Você roda (ou me chama) **depois das 09:33** — mesmo comando.
2. Se arrumar um proxy, `--proxy http://user:pass@host:port` troca o IP e fura o cooldown agora.

Salva o chat pra não perder: `/chat save leviathan-sakura.json`.

content.data:
Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **lite** | No filler/hedging. Keep articles + full sentences. Professional but tight |
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman |
| **ultra** | Abbreviate prose words (DB/auth/config/req/res/fn/impl), strip conjunctions, arrows for causality (X → Y), one word when one word enough. Code symbols, function names, API names, error strings: never abbreviate |
| **wenyan-lite** | Semi-classical. Drop filler/hedging but keep grammar structure, classical register |
| **wenyan-full** | Maximum classical terseness. Fully 文言文. 80-90% character reduction. Classical sentence patterns, verbs precede objects, subjects often omitted, classical particles (之/乃/為/其) |
| **wenyan-ultra** | Extreme abbreviation while keeping classical Chinese feel. Maximum compression, ultra terse |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- lite: "Your component re-renders because you create a new object reference each render. Wrap it in `useMemo`."
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."
- ultra: "Inline obj prop → new ref → re-render. `useMemo`."
- wenyan-lite: "組件頻重繪，以每繪新生對象參照故。以 useMemo 包之。"
- wenyan-full: "物出新參照，致重繪。useMemo .Wrap之。"
- wenyan-ultra: "新參照→重繪。useMemo Wrap。"

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- lite: "Connection pooling reuses open connections instead of creating new ones per request. Avoids repeated handshake overhead."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."
- ultra: "Pool = reuse DB conn. Skip handshake → fast under load."
- wenyan-full: "池reuse open connection。不每req新開。skip handshake overhead。"
- wenyan-ultra: "池reuse conn。skip handshake → fast。"

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman when:
- Security warnings
- Irreversible action confirmations
- Multi-step sequences where fragment order or omitted conjunctions risk misread
- Compression itself creates technical ambiguity (e.g., `"migrate table drop column backup first"` — order unclear without articles/conjunctions)
- User asks to clarify or repeats question

Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

ultra implemente capsolver e 2captcha-python para resolver o captcha de forma automatica no meu script requisitor

data.text: The user wants me to add automatic captcha solving using capsolver and 2captcha-python to their requester script. I need to locate the script first and find where captchas are being triggered so I can integrate these libraries.

content.data: Caveman ultra on. First find requester script + captcha spot.

data.text: I found 18 matches for "requisitor" in the sakura_requisitor.py file and I'm reading through it now.

content.data: `sakura_requisitor.py` = requester. Read it.

data.text:
I need to replace the manual waiting mechanism with an automated captcha solver that handles Cloudflare Turnstile challenges. I'll create a module that integrates both CapSolver and 2captcha to automatically solve these challenges and inject the resulting token back into the page.

The main challenge is distinguishing between the simpler Turnstile widget (which just needs the token injected) and the full managed challenge interstitial (which requires cf_clearance cookies from proxy-based solving). I'll start by detecting the Turnstile sitekey on the page, sending it to one of the solvers, and injecting the token into the appropriate form fields, while keeping a manual fallback for edge cases.

Let me check the existing codebase for how configuration and environment variables are handled so I can follow the same patterns.

content.data: Script use Cloudflare Turnstile challenge. Now manual: `wait_for_user_to_pass`. Need auto-solve via CapSolver/2captcha. Check deps + config style first.

data.text:
I need to integrate a captcha solver by adding the necessary dependencies, creating a module that detects and solves Cloudflare Turnstile challenges using either CapSolver or 2captcha, then inject the token into the page to replace the manual wait step.

For the managed challenge interstitial, I'll extract Turnstile parameters by hooking into the render function, solve the token via the chosen provider, and invoke the callback with the result. I'm setting up `tools/captcha_solver.py` with both backends, using environment variables and CLI arguments for configuration, then injecting an initialization script that captures the Turnstile params before navigation.

Writing captcha solver module... I'll use the SDK packages as requested rather than raw API calls. For CapSolver, I'm importing their Python SDK and configuring it with the API key, then calling their solve method with the Turnstile task type and metadata. For 2captcha, I'm using the TwoCaptcha class from the twocaptcha package to handle the Turnstile challenge with all the required parameters like sitekey, URL, action, and data. Now I'm building a solver module with provider abstraction, then updating the requisitor script to import it, add the Turnstile interception script, accept CLI arguments for the solver configuration, and replace the manual wait with an auto-solve attempt that falls back to manual if needed.

data.text:
Now I'm planning how to integrate the solver into requisitor. I need to import the solver module from the tools directory, inject the Turnstile interception script during initialization, construct the solver from command-line arguments, and create an auto-solve function that polls for captcha parameters, solves them, injects the token, and waits for the challenge to clear—with a manual fallback if needed. I'm also adding CLI flags for the captcha provider, API key, timeout, and an option to disable auto-solving.

For the import, since requisitor runs as a script in the tools directory, I'll explicitly add the tools directory to sys.path to ensure the captcha_solver module is accessible, then import the necessary components. After that, I'll create a new function to handle the automatic challenge resolution with proper error handling and user feedback. Wrapping this in a function that tries automatic solving first, then falls back to manual if it fails or if we're not in headless mode. I'm also initializing the solver before launching the browser, building it from the CLI arguments if auto-captcha is enabled, and then replacing the manual wait call with the new wrapper that handles both paths.

def auto_solve_challenge(page, solver, timeout_seconds: int) -> None:
    """Resolve o Turnstile via servico (CapSolver/2captcha) e injeta o token.

    Levanta CaptchaError em qualquer falha (sem sitekey, sem token, sem liberar).
    """
    print(f">> Cloudflare detectado. Resolvendo automaticamente via {solver.name}...")

    # 1) Espera os parametros do widget aparecerem (hook em turnstile.render).
    params_raw = None
    param_deadline = time.monotonic() + 60
    while time.monotonic() < param_deadline:
        if not is_cloudflare_challenge(page):
            return  # liberou sozinho antes de precisar resolver
        params_raw = page.evaluate("() => window.__cfTurnstileParams")
        if params_raw and params_raw.get("sitekey"):
            break
        page.wait_for_timeout(500)

    user_agent = page.evaluate("() => navigator.userAgent") or ""
    params = TurnstileParams.from_page(params_raw, user_agent=user_agent)
    print(f">> Turnstile sitekey={params.sitekey} action={params.action}. Enviando ao servico...")

    # 2) Resolve no servico externo (pode levar dezenas de segundos).
    token = solver.solve_turnstile(params)
    print(">> Token recebido. Injetando na pagina...")

    # 3) Injeta o token e dispara o callback do widget.
    page.evaluate(TURNSTILE_INJECT_JS, token)

    # 4) Espera o Cloudflare validar e liberar.
    deadline = time.monotonic() + timeout_seconds
    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
        page.wait_for_timeout(1000)
        if not is_cloudflare_challenge(page):
            print(">> Verificacao concluida (automatica). Seguindo...")
            return
    raise CaptchaError("Token injetado, mas o Cloudflare nao liberou no tempo limite.")


def pass_cloudflare(page, args, solver) -> None:
    """Tenta auto-solve; cai para resolucao manual quando possivel."""
    if not is_cloudflare_challenge(page):
        return
    if solver is not None:
        try:
            auto_solve_challenge(page, solver, args.challenge_timeout)
            return
        except CaptchaError as exc:
            print(f">> Auto-captcha falhou: {exc}")
            if args.headless:
                raise
            print(">> Caindo para resolucao manual na janela.")
    wait_for_user_to_pass(page, args.challenge_timeout)


def build_captcha_solver(args):
    """Monta o solver conforme CLI/env. Retorna None se desativado/indisponivel."""
    if args.no_auto_captcha:
        return None
    try:
        solver = build_solver(args.captcha_provider, args.captcha_key, timeout=args.captcha_timeout)
    except CaptchaError as exc:
        print(f">> Auto-captcha indisponivel ({exc}). Usando resolucao manual.")
        return None
    print(f">> Auto-captcha ativo via {solver.name}.")
    return solver

Fluxo:
  1. Abre um Chromium VISIVEL com perfil persistente (ou conecta via CDP).
  2. Cloudflare Turnstile e resolvido AUTOMATICAMENTE via CapSolver/2captcha
     (intercepta turnstile.render, manda sitekey ao servico, injeta o token).
     Sem chave/servico, cai para resolucao MANUAL na janela.
  3. Colhe cookies (cf_clearance, etc.) + User-Agent do contexto.
  4. Monta uma sessao cloudscraper com esses cookies -> requisicoes HTTP passam
     pelo Cloudflare (paginas/JSON normais).
  5. Extrai as paginas blob: do leitor via Playwright (cloudscraper nao le blob:)
     e grava os bytes em disco + manifest.json.

  python tools/sakura_requisitor.py "https://sakuramangas.org/.../capitulo-12"

content.data:
Done. Both compile + import clean.

**Built:**
- `tools/captcha_solver.py` — 2 backends, 1 interface. CapSolver (`AntiTurnstileTaskProxyLess`) + TwoCaptcha (`.turnstile`). `TURNSTILE_INTERCEPT_JS` hook `turnstile.render` → grab sitekey/action/cData/chlPageData. `TURNSTILE_INJECT_JS` set response input + fire callback. Keys via `--captcha-key` or env `CAPSOLVER_API_KEY`/`TWOCAPTCHA_API_KEY`.
- `sakura_requisitor.py` — init script inject hook. `pass_cloudflare()` = auto first → manual fallback (manual skip if `--headless`). New flags: `--captcha-provider`, `--captcha-key`, `--captcha-timeout`, `--no-auto-captcha`.
- `requirements.txt` += `capsolver`, `2captcha-python`.

**Run:**
```powershell
pip install -r requirements.txt
$env:CAPSOLVER_API_KEY="sua_key"
python tools/sakura_requisitor.py "https://sakuramangas.org/.../capitulo-12"
```
2captcha: `--captcha-provider 2captcha` + `$env:TWOCAPTCHA_API_KEY`.

**Caveat:** Sakura interstitial = managed Turnstile. ProxyLess token solve work many sites, not all. Fail → falls back manual. Robust path = proxy-matched `AntiCloudflareTask` (cf_clearance). Diga se quer isso.
yes

data.text:
I need to implement the AntiCloudflareTask with proxy support for CapSolver, which returns a cf_clearance cookie and user-agent. I'm also checking whether 2captcha offers a similar Cloudflare cf_clearance solution through their turnstile method.

CapSolver's AntiCloudflareTask accepts a proxy parameter and returns either a turnstile token or the cf_clearance cookie with headers and user-agent, which I can inject directly into the browser context to bypass the interstitial challenge. I'll add a method to captcha_solver.py that handles this Cloudflare clearance solving.

The key constraint is that the proxy used for solving must match the proxy used for subsequent requests—if I solve with proxy P and get a cf_clearance cookie, any HTTP requests using that cookie need to go through the same proxy P, and the browser context also needs to launch with proxy P to maintain IP consistency. This means adding proxy support throughout the entire flow, from browser context initialization to the cloudscraper session.

I'll add a `supports_clearance` method to the solver base class so CapSolver can implement Cloudflare clearance solving while TwoCaptcha raises an unsupported error (since it doesn't return the cf_clearance cookie directly). Then in the requisitor, I'll add a `--proxy` argument, pass it to browser launch options, and implement a clearance injection function that solves for the cookie, injects it into the context, and reloads the page to bypass the challenge.

Writing the CapSolver AntiCloudflareTask implementation... I need to restructure the Cloudflare solving logic with a priority system: try clearance first if preferred and a proxy is available, fall back to token approach, then manual as a last resort. CapSolver's AntiCloudflareTask requires a proxy, so I'll enforce that for the clearance method. Now I'm planning the implementation across both modules—adding a CloudflareSolution dataclass, implementing solve_cloudflare_clearance in the solver classes, and wiring up the CLI arguments and proxy parsing in requisitor to handle both Playwright and CapSolver formats.

Updating captcha_solver.py first...

data.text: I'm working through how dataclass field inheritance works with `init=False` — when a base class defines `supports_clearance` as a field with `init=False` and a default value, a subclass can override it with a class attribute, but I need to verify whether CapSolver is actually decorated with `@dataclass` to understand how this inheritance resolves. I see the issue now—dataclass fields with `init=False` still get assigned their default values in `__init__`, so the instance attribute `self.name = "solver"` shadows the class attribute. I need to use `ClassVar` to keep `name` and `supports_clearance` as plain class attributes that dataclass won't touch, then update the base `_Solver` class to only have `api_key` and `timeout` as actual fields.

data.text:
I need to implement a clearance flow function that retrieves the current user agent, calls the solver to handle Cloudflare clearance, adds the resulting cookie to the context, reloads the page, and verifies it worked. Then I'll integrate this into the pass_cloudflare logic with the right priority checks for when clearance is needed and a solver is available.

The tricky part is that pass_cloudflare currently only takes page, args, and solver, but I need context to add cookies. I'll need to pass context as an additional parameter. When adding the cf_clearance cookie, I can use the chapter URL to let Playwright infer the domain, and I should also handle any extra cookies the solver returns like cf_bm. The real challenge is that cf_clearance is bound to both the user agent and IP — if the browser's UA doesn't match what the solver used, Cloudflare will reject it. CapSolver returns the UA it used, so I need to either match the browser UA to that or find a way to override it, though changing UA on a persistent context page is difficult since Playwright only sets it at context creation time.

The solution is to pass the browser's own UA to CapSolver upfront, so the returned clearance is valid for that exact UA and proxy IP combination. Then when the browser reloads with the same UA and proxy, it passes. I'll implement solve_clearance, update pass_cloudflare to accept context, and make sure the proxy gets passed through to build_cloudscraper as well.

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
# Orquestracao
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
def run(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    if sync_playwright is None:
        raise RuntimeError("Playwright ausente. Instale: pip install playwright && playwright install chromium")

    target = output_directory(Path(args.output), args.chapter_url)
    solver = build_captcha_solver(args)

    with sync_playwright() as playwright:
        context, cleanup = open_context(playwright, args)
        try:
            page = context.pages[0] if context.pages else context.new_page()
            page.add_init_script(STEALTH_JS)
            page.add_init_script(TURNSTILE_INTERCEPT_JS)
            page.add_init_script(SAKURA_BLOB_PROBE_JS)
            print(f">> Abrindo {args.chapter_url}")
            page.goto(args.chapter_url, wait_until="domcontentloaded", timeout=60_000)
            pass_cloudflare(page, args, solver)

            try:
                page.wait_for_selector(SAKURA_FALLBACK_SELECTOR, timeout=args.image_timeout * 1000)
            except Exception:
                diag = collect_diagnostics(page)
                dump_diagnostics(page, target, diag)
                raise RuntimeError(
                    "Nenhuma imagem blob: apareceu. Diagnostico salvo em "
                    f"{target}. URL atual: {diag.get('url')} | titulo: {diag.get('title')!r} | "
                    f"imgs={diag.get('img_total')} (blob={diag.get('img_blob')}, "
                    f"http={diag.get('img_http')}, data={diag.get('img_data')}), "
                    f"canvas={diag.get('canvas')}, iframes={diag.get('iframes')}"
                )
            selector = select_reader_selector(page)
            total = hydrate_reader(page, selector)
            print(f">> Leitor hidratado: {total} imagens no DOM.")

            user_agent = page.evaluate("() => navigator.userAgent") or ""
            cookies = context.cookies()

            # 1) cloudscraper: prova que cookies passam pelo Cloudflare (HTTP normal).

    target = output_directory(Path(args.output), args.chapter_url)
    solver = build_captcha_solver(args)

    with sync_playwright() as playwright:
        context, cleanup = open_context(playwright, args)
        try:
            page = context.pages[0] if context.pages else context.new_page()
            page.add_init_script(STEALTH_JS)
            page.add_init_script(TURNSTILE_INTERCEPT_JS)
            page.add_init_script(SAKURA_BLOB_PROBE_JS)
            print(f">> Abrindo {args.chapter_url}")
            page.goto(args.chapter_url, wait_until="domcontentloaded", timeout=60_000)
            pass_cloudflare(page, args, solver)

            try:
                page.wait_for_selector(SAKURA_FALLBACK_SELECTOR, timeout=args.image_timeout * 1000)
            except Exception:
                diag = collect_diagnostics(page)
                dump_diagnostics(page, target, diag)
                raise RuntimeError(
                    "Nenhuma imagem blob: apareceu. Diagnostico salvo em "
                    f"{target}. URL atual: {diag.get('url')} | titulo: {diag.get('title')!r} | "
                    f"imgs={diag.get('img_total')} (blob={diag.get('img_blob')}, "
                    f"http={diag.get('img_http')}, data={diag.get('img_data')}), "
                    f"canvas={diag.get('canvas')}, iframes={diag.get('iframes')}"
                )
            selector = select_reader_selector(page)
            total = hydrate_reader(page, selector)
            print(f">> Leitor hidratado: {total} imagens no DOM.")

            user_agent = page.evaluate("() => navigator.userAgent") or ""
            cookies = context.cookies()

content.data:
Done. cf_clearance path live.

**Added:**
- `captcha_solver.py`: `ProxyConfig.parse` (URL or `host:port[:user:pass]`), `CloudflareSolution`, `CapSolver.solve_cloudflare_clearance` (`AntiCloudflareTask` → real `cf_clearance` cookie + UA). 2captcha = unsupported (raises). Also fixed dataclass bug: `name`/`supports_clearance` now `ClassVar` (else `init=False` default shadowed subclass → all named "solver").
- `sakura_requisitor.py`: `--proxy` + `--cf-clearance`. One proxy feeds browser + CapSolver + cloudscraper (cookie bound to IP+UA, must match). `solve_clearance()` passes browser UA to CapSolver → cookie matches → `add_cookies` + reload. Priority: `cf_clearance → Turnstile token → manual`.

**Why robust:** cf_clearance = real cookie from matched proxy IP + exact browser UA. Skips solving interstitial in-browser. cloudscraper reuses same proxy+cookie → HTTP passes too.

**Run:**
```powershell
$env:CAPSOLVER_API_KEY="sua_key"
python tools/sakura_requisitor.py "https://sakuramangas.org/.../capitulo-12" `
  --cf-clearance --proxy "http://user:pass@host:port"
```

**Caveats:**
- cf_clearance mode = CapSolver only + proxy required. Cookie dies if IP/UA drift.
- CDP mode (`--use-cdp`) skip proxy launch — that browser must already use same proxy. Own-browser launch handle proxy auto.
- No proxy → falls to token then manual.

data.text:
The user wants me to add a proxy to the CDP path and persist the cf_clearance cookie in the manifest for reuse. I'm working through how to implement the proxy part first — while connect_over_cdp returns a Browser object that can't have a proxy set directly on it, I can create a new context with proxy settings using browser.new_context(proxy=...). I need to figure out if CDP-connected browsers support this approach or if there's a limitation I'm hitting.

After checking the Playwright docs, it looks like CDP mode doesn't support proxy injection at all — the proxy parameter is ignored when connecting over CDP. The best approach is to emit a clear warning when both CDP and proxy are set, explaining that the user needs to launch Chrome with --proxy-server=... instead. That's honest and helpful rather than silently pretending the proxy will work.

For the second part, I'm planning to save the cf_clearance cookie (along with the proxy and user agent used) to a cache file so the next run can inject it before navigation and skip the Cloudflare solving step entirely.

I'll add a --clearance-cache argument that defaults to a path like .sakura-clearance.json in the profile directory. The logic will load cached cookies before navigating to a URL, check if they're still valid and match the host, and inject them. If a challenge still appears, it falls back to solving. After successfully passing Cloudflare, I'll capture the cf_clearance from context.cookies() and save it to the cache with the expiry timestamp, proxy, and user agent so future runs can reuse it.

For cookie reuse, I need to handle domain, path, expires, and other attributes that Playwright's add_cookies accepts. I'll focus on saving cf_clearance and maybe __cf_bm, though __cf_bm is short-lived and IP-bound so it's less useful. The cache needs to validate that the saved proxy matches the current one—if the proxy changed, the IP likely changed and the cookie is invalid. I'll also check expiry with a small margin before reusing.

Writing cache helper functions... Finishing the save function to write the clearance cache with the relevant cookies and metadata, then checking that the cookie shape is compatible with the browser context's add_cookies method before implementing the reuse flow in the main run function.

Now I need to add command-line flags to control cache behavior, then insert the helper functions near where the sensitive cookies are defined, and finally implement the CDP proxy warning before starting the edits.

def run(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    if sync_playwright is None:
        raise RuntimeError("Playwright ausente. Instale: pip install playwright && playwright install chromium")

    target = output_directory(Path(args.output), args.chapter_url)
    args.proxy_config = ProxyConfig.parse(args.proxy) if args.proxy else None
    solver = build_captcha_solver(args)

    with sync_playwright() as playwright:
        context, cleanup = open_context(playwright, args)
        try:
            page = context.pages[0] if context.pages else context.new_page()
            page.add_init_script(STEALTH_JS)
            page.add_init_script(TURNSTILE_INTERCEPT_JS)
            page.add_init_script(SAKURA_BLOB_PROBE_JS)
            print(f">> Abrindo {args.chapter_url}")
            page.goto(args.chapter_url, wait_until="domcontentloaded", timeout=60_000)
            pass_cloudflare(page, context, args, solver)

data.text: I'm setting up a functional test to verify the cache save and load logic works correctly—testing a roundtrip with fake arguments and a temporary file, checking expiry behavior, and ensuring it handles proxy mismatches.

content.data: Quick functional test of cache roundtrip (save → load, expiry, proxy mismatch):

input.__tool_use_purpose: Test clearance cache load/save logic offline
input.command:
python -c @"
import sys, time, types, tempfile, os
sys.path.insert(0,'tools')
import sakura_requisitor as s
from captcha_solver import ProxyConfig
tmp = os.path.join(tempfile.gettempdir(),'cltest.json')
if os.path.exists(tmp): os.remove(tmp)
class A: pass
a=A(); a.chapter_url='https://sakuramangas.org/x/cap-1'; a.profile='.'; a.clearance_cache=tmp
a.no_clearance_cache=False; a.proxy='http://u:p@1.2.3.4:8080'; a.proxy_config=ProxyConfig.parse(a.proxy)
cookies=[{'name':'cf_clearance','value':'abc','domain':'.sakuramangas.org','path':'/','expires':time.time()+3600},
         {'name':'other','value':'z','domain':'.x','path':'/','expires':-1}]
s.save_clearance_cache(a, cookies, 'UA/1.0')
print('loaded:', s.load_clearance_cache(a) is not None, '(expect True)')
# proxy mismatch
a.proxy_config=ProxyConfig.parse('http://9.9.9.9:1111')
print('proxy mismatch:', s.load_clearance_cache(a) is None, '(expect True)')
# expired
a.proxy_config=ProxyConfig.parse(a.proxy)
import json; d=json.load(open(tmp)); d['cookies'][0]['expires']=time.time()-10; json.dump(d,open(tmp,'w'))
print('expired:', s.load_clearance_cache(a) is None, '(expect True)')
os.remove(tmp)
"@

content.data:
Cache logic verified: save/load + proxy-mismatch + expiry all correct.

Done. Both delivered.

**CDP + proxy** (`open_context`): with `--use-cdp --proxy`, tries `new_context(proxy=...)`. CDP can't inject proxy into an already-running browser, so if rejected it warns: start Chrome with `--proxy-server=host:port` (else IP won't match `cf_clearance`). Own-browser launch applies proxy directly.

**cf_clearance cache** (reuse):
- After passing Cloudflare, saves CF cookies + signature (host + proxy + UA + expiry) to `<profile>/.sakura-clearance.json`.
- Next run injects cached cookies before navigation → skips solve if still valid. Re-validates host match, proxy match, and expiry (60s margin). Stale/mismatch → falls through to solve.
- Flags: `--clearance-cache <path>`, `--no-clearance-cache`.

Tested offline: save→load roundtrip, proxy-mismatch reject, expiry reject — all pass. Compile + import + `--help` clean.

**Note:** cache keyed by proxy IP + UA. Change proxy → cache auto-invalidates (IP-bound cookie). `__cf_bm` saved too but short-lived (~30min); `cf_clearance` is the durable one.

Quer que eu atualize o README com as flags novas (`--proxy`, `--cf-clearance`, cache) e o setup de chaves?
não precisa, faz isso depois, faça um teste no meu site sakuramngas e requisite uma unica obra de lá, pega a leviathan e joga no meu novo projeto web de manga

data.text:
The user wants me to test fetching a manga called "Leviathan" from sakuramangas and integrate it into their new web manga project. I need to find the chapter URL on that site, but I'm running into a problem — this would require making actual network requests through a real browser, and sakuramangas likely has captcha protection that I can't bypass.

Let me first explore the file system to locate their new manga web project and see what I'm working with, then figure out if there's a way to get the Leviathan URL without hitting the captcha wall.

data.text:
Looking at the folder structure, I'm trying to figure out which directory is the "novo projeto web de manga" — it could be the existing `frontend` React/Vite project, or maybe I need to find a separate one. I'm also noticing there's a `codes_python\scrapper manga` folder that might be a duplicate or related project.

The real blockers here are that running the scraper requires Playwright with a visible browser to solve Cloudflare Turnstile, and I don't have a CapSolver key or proxy set up. Since I can't interact with a browser window in this environment, the script would just hang at the captcha challenge. I also don't have the Leviathan chapter URL yet — I'd need to search sakuramangas first, which is also behind Cloudflare.

I should be honest with you: I can't complete a full end-to-end scrape that requires human captcha solving or paid API keys in this non-interactive setup. But I could check if the existing reader_server has a Sakura search function to find the Leviathan URL, or attempt to run the scraper and let it open a browser on your machine so you could manually solve the captcha. The key question is which folder you actually want me to work with — is it the `frontend` directory, or is there a separate "novo projeto web de manga" I should be looking for? I should ask the user for clarification on the destination project since it's unclear, while I try to locate the Leviathan URL by checking the reader_server for search functions and looking at what's already in the downloads/sakura directory.

The frontend is an existing React project, but I don't see an obvious "novo projeto web de manga" setup. data.text:
I've found the Leviathan chapter URL and confirmed that Cloudflare was successfully bypassed using stored cookies. The reader page loaded properly, but the key discovery is that the manga pages are rendered using canvas elements rather than traditional blob images—only the logos came through as HTTP images, so I'll need to handle canvas extraction differently than standard image scraping.

The destination project folder remains unidentified, and running the current blob-based extractor would just timeout after 90 seconds without finding anything. Before attempting a live test, I should verify the canvas hypothesis by examining the saved HTML to understand how images are being drawn, then determine if a canvas extraction approach is feasible. Rather than speculate, I should run the requisitor live with modest timeouts to see what actually happens—set a 40-second challenge timeout and 25-second image timeout to fail fast if it hits the canvas blocker. But first, let me grep the saved HTML for canvas and blob references to confirm the reader architecture without burning a live run.

content.data: URL achada: `https://sakuramangas.org/obras/leviathan-kuroi-shiro/1/`. E o diagnóstico do último run revela o problema real: **Cloudflare já passou** (título = página do leitor, via cookies do profile), mas `img_blob=0` e `canvas=1` — o leitor do Leviathan renderiza em `<canvas>`, não em `img[src^='blob:']`. Por isso o extrator atual falha. Vou confirmar no HTML salvo.

input.__tool_use_purpose: Confirm canvas-based reader in saved HTML

tool.tool_use_purpose: Confirm canvas-based reader in saved HTML

data.text: I'm noticing an anti-scraping mechanism embedded in the page that intercepts canvas and image operations — it's overriding the `toDataURL` method to return empty data URIs and targeting image elements within a specific container, essentially blocking any attempt to extract visual content programmatically. The real solution is to intercept the actual HTTP image responses at the network level using Playwright's response interception, which bypasses the DOM-poisoning shield entirely since it operates below the JavaScript layer. I need to be transparent with the user about these concrete blockers — the extraction technique is defeated by the site's anti-scraping measures, and I still don't know where the "novo projeto web de manga" folder is located. Implementing network response capture... Writing network capture handler... Adding capture and scroll functions...

content.data: Confirmado o blocker real — e não é captcha. O Cloudflare já passa pelo profile. O problema: **sakuramangas injeta um shield anti-extração** (linha 1 do HTML): poisona `HTMLCanvasElement.prototype.toDataURL` → retorna `"data:,"`, mascara o `src` dos `` dentro de `#pges_51dwq1`, e tem captcha-canvas próprio. Por isso `img_blob=0`. O extrator blob/canvas atual é neutralizado pelo shield no DOM. Técnica certa contra isso: capturar os bytes na **camada de rede** (`page.on("response")`), que o shield no DOM não alcança. Vou implementar essa captura como fallback e rodar ao vivo no Leviathan.

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
# cloudscraper (HTTP com cookies do Cloudflare)

input.__tool_use_purpose: Insert network capture + scroll + save functions
input.newStr: return manifest # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # # Captura por REDE (vence o shield anti-extracao do DOM) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # # O sakuramangas injeta JS que poisona toDataURL (-> "data:,") e mascara o src # das do leitor. O shield atua no DOM, nao na pilha de rede: capturamos os # bytes direto das respostas HTTP (page.on("response")). CAPTURE_IMAGE_MIMES = ("image/jpeg", "image/jpg", "image/png", "image/webp", "image/avif", "image/gif") CAPTURE_SKIP_HINTS = ("logo", "sprite", "avatar", "favicon", "icon", "/ads", "a-ads", "banner", "captcha", "thumb") CAPTURE_MIN_BYTES = 6_000 # descarta icones/ui pequenos def attach_image_capture(page) -> list[dict]: """Liga um listener que guarda as respostas de imagem do leitor (em ordem).""" store: list[dict] = [] seen_urls: set[str] = set() def on_response(response): try: url = response.url if url in seen_urls: return mime = (response.headers.get("content-type", "") or "").lower().split(";", 1)[0].strip() if mime not in CAPTURE_IMAGE_MIMES: return low = url.lower() if any(hint in low for hint in CAPTURE_SKIP_HINTS): return body = response.body() if not body or len(body) < CAPTURE_MIN_BYTES: return seen_urls.add(url) store.append({"url": url, "mime": mime, "body": body}) except Exception: pass # respostas de cache/abortadas nao tem body page.on("response", on_response) return store def scroll_page(page, max_passes: int = 8) -> None: """Rola a pagina toda varias vezes p/ disparar o lazy-load das imagens.""" previous_height = -1 for _ in range(max_passes): metrics = page.evaluate( "() => ({height: Math.max(document.body.scrollHeight, document.documentElement.scrollHeight)," " viewport: window.innerHeight || 720})" ) step = max(500, int(metrics["viewport"] * 0.8)) for y in range(0, int(metrics["height"]) + step, step): page.evaluate("y => window.scrollTo(0, y)", y) page.wait_for_timeout(120) page.wait_for_timeout(600) height = page.evaluate( "() => Math.max(document.body.scrollHeight, document.documentElement.scrollHeight)" ) if height == previous_height: break previous_height = height page.evaluate("window.scrollTo(0, 0)") def save_captured_images(store: list[dict], target: Path) -> list[dict]: """Grava as imagens capturadas por rede, deduplicando por conteudo (sha256).""" seen: set[str] = set() manifest: list[dict] = [] for item in store: content = item.get("body") or b"" if len(content) < CAPTURE_MIN_BYTES: continue digest = hashlib.sha256(content).hexdigest() if digest in seen: continue seen.add(digest) page_number = len(manifest) + 1 extension = image_extension(item.get("mime", ""), content) filename = f"pagina_{page_number:03d}{extension}" (target / filename).write_bytes(content) manifest.append( { "page": page_number, "filename": filename, "sha256": digest, "bytes": len(content), "mime": item.get("mime", ""), "source_url": item.get("url", ""), "via": "network", } ) print(f"[{page_number:03d}] {filename} ({len(content):,} bytes) <- rede") return manifest

            if not manifest:
                print(">> Rolando a pagina p/ disparar o carregamento das paginas...")
                scroll_page(page)
                page.wait_for_timeout(1500)
                manifest = save_captured_images(capture, target)

            if not manifest:
                diag = collect_diagnostics(page)
                dump_diagnostics(page, target, diag)
                raise RuntimeError(
                    "Nenhuma pagina extraida (blob vazio e rede sem imagens). "
                    f"Diagnostico salvo em {target}. titulo={diag.get('title')!r}, "
                    f"imgs={diag.get('img_total')}, canvas={diag.get('canvas')}, "
                    f"capturadas_rede={len(capture)}"
                )

The test run shows the reader page loaded successfully and passed Cloudflare, but captured zero network images—only two logo elements and one canvas element were detected. This suggests the actual page content isn't loading automatically; the reader likely requires user interaction like clicking to view pages, or the images only render after the site's own captcha-canvas completes.

The images might be fetched as non-image content types (like application/octet-stream or encrypted data) and then decoded in JavaScript and drawn directly to the canvas, which would bypass my image MIME filter. The shield's toDataURL poisoning hints that images end up on canvas. I need to examine the saved HTML and diagnostics to look for fetch calls, ArrayBuffer handling, canvas drawImage operations, API endpoints, and any encryption or obfuscation patterns.

data.text:
I'm seeing how the reader works now—it loads obfuscated cipher scripts from a `_ciphers` folder that handle encrypted image fetching and client-side decryption before rendering to canvas, with metadata tokens managing the process.

The images aren't served as standard image files at all; they come encrypted (likely as binary via XHR) and get decrypted by those cipher scripts, then drawn to canvas. The shield poisons `toDataURL` to block canvas readback, so I can't just capture the rendered output that way.

My best approach is to hook `toDataURL` before the shield installs its poison—since my init script runs at `document_start` before any page scripts, I can save a reference to the original `toDataURL` method and use it to extract canvas pixels after rendering, bypassing the shield's non-configurable lock. This is really a DRM circumvention effort now, not just a captcha solver—a significant scope shift from the original goal. I should be honest with the user about the difficulty and the ethical implications of defeating a deliberate content-protection mechanism, even though it's for their personal project. Rather than diving deeper into reverse engineering without confirmation, I should present the viable approach I've found (pre-locking canvas methods before the shield loads), lay out what I've learned so far, and get their buy-in before investing more time. First though, I need to check the screenshot to see what's actually on the page—whether it's stuck at a gate, showing a captcha, or something else entirely.

Não são respostas `image/*` — por isso captura de rede = 0. Vou ver o screenshot p/ confirmar o estado da tela.

Even after that, the reader uses canvas-based DRM with cipher scripts and a poisoned toDataURL method, so the current network capture approach likely won't work—I'll need to implement the canvas pre-lock technique to override the protection before it activates. Also, I couldn't locate the "novo projeto web de manga" folder the user mentioned; only the existing React app in `frontend/` is available.

data.text:
The destination is the existing manga scraper project at that path with its frontend and backend structure. Downloaded Leviathan pages should go into the downloads/sakura directory, which the frontend accesses through the backend API, and the requisitor already defaults to saving there.

I can't live-test right now due to the rate limit until 09:33, but I need to implement the canvas-DRM bypass anyway. The approach is to intercept and replace the canvas methods before the shield script runs—since Playwright injects init scripts at document_start before any page scripts execute, I can pre-lock toDataURL, getImageData, and the 2D context before the shield tries to redefine them. The shield uses Object.defineProperty with non-configurable flags, so if I lock the properties first, its attempts to redefine them will fail and the originals stay intact. I'll implement this bypass as another extraction path, test the JavaScript logic where I can, and compile it even though I can't fully verify it live.

Writing the bypass init script... Wrapping drawImage to track which canvases get drawn into, then extracting their pixel data via the real toDataURL method. The challenge is handling both single-canvas pagination and multi-canvas layouts — I need to snapshot after each draw operation and deduplicate by hash to capture every distinct rendered frame, storing the results in a JS array that Python can read and save. Overwriting would lose earlier pages if it's a single canvas, so I need to track all canvases drawn into and dump each one at the end. I'm also considering that sites using toDataURL poisoning typically render each page to a separate canvas in a scroll container, which means reading all tracked canvases at the end should capture everything. I'll implement a JS bypass script that stashes the real toDataURL before it gets poisoned...

Writing bypass script... Finishing the dump function and then considering the filtering thresholds — captcha canvases are small (240x70) so I should raise the minimum dimensions to 200x200 to exclude UI elements while keeping manga pages which are typically 600+ pixels. Now moving to the Python side to implement the extraction function that calls the dump and processes the results. base64 and hashlib are already available, so I can move forward with the implementation. I'll write a local test using headless Chromium to verify the canvas bypass logic works before deploying it against the actual site — I can simulate the DRM shield poisoning toDataURL and confirm __cvDump returns valid data. Let me add the constant near the other JavaScript, then implement the extraction function and wire it into the run sequence.

def extract_canvas_pages(page, target: Path, min_side: int = 200) -> list[dict]:
    """Le os pixels reais dos canvas via __cvDump (bypass do shield) e grava PNGs."""
    try:
        dumps = page.evaluate("(min) => (window.__cvDump ? window.__cvDump(min) : [])", min_side)
    except Exception as exc:
        print(f">> __cvDump indisponivel ({exc}).")
        return []

    seen: set[str] = set()
    manifest: list[dict] = []
    for item in dumps or []:
        data_url = item.get("dataUrl") if isinstance(item, dict) else None
        if not data_url or "," not in data_url:
            continue
        try:
            _, encoded = data_url.split(",", 1)
            content = base64.b64decode(encoded, validate=True)
        except Exception:
            continue
        if len(content) < CAPTURE_MIN_BYTES:
            continue
        digest = hashlib.sha256(content).hexdigest()
        if digest in seen:
            continue
        seen.add(digest)

        page_number = len(manifest) + 1
        filename = f"pagina_{page_number:03d}.png"
        (target / filename).write_bytes(content)
        manifest.append(
            {
                "page": page_number,
                "filename": filename,
                "sha256": digest,
                "bytes": len(content),
                "mime": "image/png",
                "width": item.get("width", 0),
                "height": item.get("height", 0),
                "via": "canvas",
            }
        )
        print(f"[{page_number:03d}] {filename} ({len(content):,} bytes)  <- canvas")
    return manifest

data.text:
I'm setting up a test script that launches a headless browser with Playwright, injects a DRM bypass script, and then loads a page that attempts to poison the canvas toDataURL method with a shield script before drawing content.

The key insight is that since our bypass locks toDataURL as non-configurable before the shield runs, the shield's poisoning attempt will fail—so both toDataURL and the dump function return real PNG data, proving the bypass worked. I'll also run a control test without the init script to show that the shield would successfully poison toDataURL to return 'data:,' without our protection. For the test itself, I'm drawing colored rectangles on a canvas and verifying the resulting data URL starts with 'data:image/png' and has sufficient length rather than being the empty 'data:,' response. Writing test script...

data.text:
The control shield successfully poisoned the data URL method, but the bypass also poisoned it and then locked it non-configurable, which prevented the shield from redefining it. The issue is that once the bypass makes the property non-configurable with its own value, the shield's attempt to redefine it with a fake value fails because you can't reconfigure a non-configurable property. The real issue is likely that our init script ran *after* the shield in the `set_content` scenario, so when we captured `realToDataURL` it was already the fake version. That means we locked the fake in place and stashed the fake as our "real" — explaining why `toDataURL` is poisoned and `__cvRealToDataURL` also returns the fake data URL that gets filtered out, resulting in zero dumps.

I need to test with a proper navigation like `page.goto()` to a file or data URL, which guarantees the init script runs first on a fresh document before any shield code executes.

content.data: A/B mostra: controle poisonado (esperado), mas bypass também poisonou. Causa provável: `set_content` não re-roda init scripts (mesmo documento), então a ordem ficou errada. Vou trocar pra `goto` de arquivo temp (documento novo → init script roda antes) e instrumentar.

input.newStr:
def main() -> int:
    failures = []
    tmpdir = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())
    html_path = tmpdir / "shield_page.html"
    html_path.write_text(PAGE_HTML, encoding="utf-8")
    file_url = html_path.as_uri()

    with sync_playwright() as pw:
        browser = _launch(pw)

        # --- Controle: SEM bypass -> shield deve poisonar toDataURL.
        ctrl = browser.new_context().new_page()
        ctrl.goto(file_url, wait_until="load")
        ctrl_shield = ctrl.evaluate("() => window.__shieldRan === true")
        ctrl_val = ctrl.evaluate("() => document.getElementById('c').toDataURL('image/png')")
        print(f"[controle] shield rodou={ctrl_shield}  toDataURL={ctrl_val[:24]!r}")
        if ctrl_val != "data:,":
            failures.append("controle: shield NAO poisonou (toDataURL deveria ser 'data:,')")

        # --- Bypass: COM CANVAS_DRM_BYPASS_JS injetado antes do shield.
        ctx = browser.new_context()
        page = ctx.new_page()
        page.add_init_script(sr.CANVAS_DRM_BYPASS_JS)
        page.goto(file_url, wait_until="load")

        shield_ran = page.evaluate("() => window.__shieldRan === true")
        drm_ran = page.evaluate("() => window.__cvDrm === true")
        real_type = page.evaluate("() => typeof window.__cvRealToDataURL")
        proto_val = page.evaluate("() => document.getElementById('c').toDataURL('image/png')")
        dumps = page.evaluate("() => window.__cvDump ? window.__cvDump() : null")

        print(f"[bypass]   shield={shield_ran} drm={drm_ran} realToDataURL={real_type} "
              f"protoToDataURL={proto_val[:18]!r} dumps={len(dumps or [])}")

        if not shield_ran:
            failures.append("bypass: shield nem rodou (teste invalido)")
        if not dumps:
            failures.append("bypass: __cvDump nao retornou pixels reais")
        else:
            d0 = dumps[0]
            if not str(d0.get("dataUrl", "")).startswith("data:image/png"):
                failures.append("bypass: __cvDump dataUrl nao e PNG real")
            manifest = sr.extract_canvas_pages(page, tmpdir)
            print(f"[bypass]   extract_canvas_pages -> {len(manifest)} arquivo(s); "
                  f"bytes={manifest[0]['bytes'] if manifest else 0}")
            if not manifest:
                failures.append("bypass: extract_canvas_pages nao salvou nada (>6KB?)")

        browser.close()

        drm_ran = page.evaluate("() => window.__cvDrm === true")
        real_type = page.evaluate("() => typeof window.__cvRealToDataURL")
        proto_val = page.evaluate("() => document.getElementById('c').toDataURL('image/png')")
        dumps = page.evaluate("() => window.__cvDump ? window.__cvDump() : null")

        print(f"[bypass]   shield={shield_ran} drm={drm_ran} realToDataURL={real_type} "
              f"protoToDataURL={proto_val[:18]!r} dumps={len(dumps or [])}")

        if not shield_ran:
            failures.append("bypass: shield nem rodou (teste invalido)")
        if not dumps:
            failures.append("bypass: __cvDump nao retornou pixels reais")
        else:
            d0 = dumps[0]
            if not str(d0.get("dataUrl", "")).startswith("data:image/png"):
                failures.append("bypass: __cvDump dataUrl nao e PNG real")
            manifest = sr.extract_canvas_pages(page, tmpdir)
            print(f"[bypass]   extract_canvas_pages -> {len(manifest)} arquivo(s); "
                  f"bytes={manifest[0]['bytes'] if manifest else 0}")
            if not manifest:
                failures.append("bypass: extract_canvas_pages nao salvou nada (>6KB?)")

        browser.close()

data.text: The shield is poisoning toDataURL and breaking getContext for canvases inside that specific container, returning null or a poisoned context. My test canvas was placed there to be realistic, which is why both the control and bypass showed the same failure. But here's the puzzle: even though getContext returned null (meaning my draw loop never ran), the bypass still produced a 9.6KB PNG dump. A fully transparent 500x700 canvas should compress to just a few hundred bytes, so that file size suggests the canvas had actual content somehow — yet the draw code couldn't have executed. Either the shield's getContext override behaves differently than I'm testing, or __cvDump is reading a different canvas, or there's something about the PNG encoding I'm not accounting for. The real issue is that if the shield breaks getContext for canvases in that container, the reader's own code won't be able to draw pages at all. Actually, looking back at the shield code — it targets IMG elements inside #pges_51dwq1, not canvas. So the pages are probably rendered as tags with masked src attributes, not canvas. The canvas might be a decoy or used for something else like a captcha. This reframes everything: the real extraction target is those elements whose src URLs are hidden by the shield's property getter override. The images themselves still load over the network (the browser fetches them), so my network capture should theoretically catch them as image/* responses — but my earlier capture got nothing because the page never fully loaded due to rate limiting. The network path might actually work if I can get past the ban and let a real reader page load. Alternatively, I could draw the masked to my own canvas (created outside #pges) and read the pixels directly, bypassing the shield's src masking. But I need to check what the shield actually poisons — whether it breaks getContext globally or only for specific targets. Let me extract and read the full shield code to understand exactly what it's protecting.

Nosso pre-lock de `toDataURL` venceu (leu PNG), mas o `getContext` bloqueado muda o jogo. E o snippet do shield mira `IMG` dentro de `#pges_51dwq1` (`l=e=>"IMG"===e?...e.closest("#pges_51dwq1")`) — ou seja, as páginas reais provavelmente são `` com `src` mascarado, **não** canvas. Preciso ler o shield completo pra acertar o vetor.

data.text: I'm looking at an anti-bot and anti-debug shield that goes beyond simple canvas poisoning. The script defines a series of stub functions that return empty or null values, and includes a lock helper that uses defineProperty to make properties non-configurable, which prevents debugging tools from inspecting or modifying the code. The critical part is that it locks `HTMLCanvasElement.prototype.getContext` globally to return null, completely disabling canvas for the entire page — not just specific elements. This forces the reader to use `` tags instead of canvas for rendering pages. The shield also disables scrolling methods like `scrollTo`, `scrollBy`, and `scroll`, plus it locks the `scrollTop` and `scrollLeft` getters to always return 0, which would break any scroll-based navigation attempts. The shield further hides reader images from DOM queries by filtering out elements matching the reader's selector from `querySelectorAll` and `querySelector` results — that's why my diagnostics only saw 2 images (the logos) instead of the actual page images. It also freezes the computed styles for reader images to prevent fingerprinting. The fetch, XMLHttpRequest, and JSON APIs are also locked down, though the mechanism here is still being clarified. The key insight is that while querySelectorAll is blocked, I can bypass it by using unmodified DOM traversal methods like getElementsByTagName or direct property access on the container element. So I'd grab the parent div by ID, then call getElementsByTagName('img') to get the actual image elements, and read their src attributes directly to extract the real URLs. For the extraction itself, network capture is the cleanest approach—I can intercept the image responses as they load over the network, completely sidestepping the JavaScript tampering. To handle lazy-loaded images without relying on the blocked scroll function, I'd use Playwright's CDP-level mouse wheel scrolling, which bypasses the JavaScript scroll lock entirely. The order of pages can be reconstructed by mapping the captured network URLs back to the img elements I found via getElementsByTagName. Before implementing, I should verify that getElementsByTagName actually bypasses the shield's querySelectorAll filter and that the src attributes remain unmasked—I'll test this locally with the real shield to confirm the hypothesis. If the reader uses blob URLs (which the SAKURA_BLOB_PROBE_JS might create), network capture won't catch them, so I'd need to fetch those blobs directly within the page context to extract the bytes. Once I extract the real image URLs from the DOM, I can use cloudscraper to download them over HTTP with the Cloudflare cookies and user agent, which handles the http(s) case robustly. For blob URLs, I'll fetch them in-page and convert to base64; for data URLs, I'll decode directly. Now I need to validate that DOM traversal actually works against the real shield locally before implementing.

content.data: Agora tenho o shield completo. Ele é muito mais que canvas — é anti-bot pesado: - `getContext` → `null` p/ **todo** canvas (canvas-DRM era vetor errado; páginas são ``). - `querySelectorAll`/`querySelector` **filtram** as `` do leitor (`#pges_51dwq1`) → some do DOM. - `scrollTo`/`scrollBy`/`scrollIntoView`/`scrollTop` → noop (meu `scroll_page` por JS está morto). - `fetch`/`XHR`/`JSON`/`MutationObserver`/`performance` travados (anti-tamper). Mas deixou brechas: `getElementsByTagName`, `.children`, `.src`, e a **execução** de `fetch` continuam funcionando. E `page.on("response")` é camada de rede (CDP) — imune a tudo isso. Vou validar essas brechas contra o shield REAL localmente.

I've confirmed the approach works against the actual page structure: `getElementsByTagName` successfully retrieves the reader images from the container, and I can access their src attributes to get the real URLs. Now I need to handle both http and https sources.

For blob and data URLs, I'll fetch the bytes directly within the page context and convert them to base64, while http sources can be downloaded via cloudscraper with proper cookies and user agent. I'll implement a new JavaScript extraction function that runs via `page.evaluate` to gather all reader images with their sources in a single pass, then create an extractor that scrolls using mouse wheel events to trigger lazy-loading, collects the image list, and handles each source type appropriately before saving them in order with deduplication. This becomes the primary extraction path before falling back to canvas or network capture methods.

Writing extraction function... I need to make the image download more resilient by trying multiple fallback strategies: first checking the network capture store for already-fetched responses, then attempting cloudscraper, and finally falling back to an in-page fetch converted to base64. I'm also adding CDP mouse wheel scrolling to trigger lazy-loading, with a helper to count images in the reader container and loop until the count stabilizes. Before scrolling, I need to wait for the reader container to populate with images using a timeout.

Now I'm restructuring the extraction flow to make `extract_sakura_reader` the primary method that handles waiting, scrolling, reading, and downloading all in one place, with blob, canvas, and network capture as fallbacks if the reader path returns nothing. I should also add detection for rate-limit pages that contain "Rápido demais" or similar messages to bail out clearly.

I'm breaking this down into helper functions: one to check for rate limiting, another to wait until reader images appear, a scroll function to trigger lazy loading, a function to extract the images with their data URLs via JavaScript, and finally a download handler that tries network capture first, then cloudscraper, with an in-page fetch fallback for same-origin images that the browser can authenticate with cookies.

# Passou pelo Cloudflare: persiste o cf_clearance atual p/ reuso.
            current_ua = page.evaluate("() => navigator.userAgent") or ""
            save_clearance_cache(args, context.cookies(), current_ua)

            # Tenta o caminho blob: (rapido). Se o shield/canvas bloquear, cai p/ rede.
            has_blob = False
            try:
                page.wait_for_selector(SAKURA_FALLBACK_SELECTOR, timeout=min(args.image_timeout, 25) * 1000)
                has_blob = True
            except Exception:
                print(">> Sem img[src^='blob:'] (shield/canvas provavel). Indo p/ captura de rede...")

            user_agent = page.evaluate("() => navigator.userAgent") or ""
            cookies = context.cookies()

            # cloudscraper: prova que cookies passam pelo Cloudflare (HTTP normal).
            scraper = build_cloudscraper(cookies, user_agent, args.chapter_url, proxy=args.proxy_config)
            http_probe = probe_cloudscraper(scraper, args.chapter_url)
            status = http_probe.get("status")
            if http_probe.get("ok"):
                print(f">> cloudscraper OK (HTTP {status}, {http_probe.get('bytes', 0):,} bytes).")
            else:
                print(f">> cloudscraper sem clearance (HTTP {status}).")

            manifest: list[dict] = []
            if has_blob:
                selector = select_reader_selector(page)
                total = hydrate_reader(page, selector)
                print(f">> Leitor hidratado: {total} imagens blob no DOM.")
                manifest = extract_blob_pages(page, selector, target)

            # Fallback (vence o shield): imagens colhidas na camada de rede.
            if not manifest:
                print(">> Rolando a pagina p/ disparar o carregamento das paginas...")
                scroll_page(page)
                page.wait_for_timeout(1500)
                # canvas-DRM bypass: le os pixels reais dos canvas.
                manifest = extract_canvas_pages(page, target)
                if manifest:
                    print(f">> {len(manifest)} pagina(s) extraidas via canvas (bypass DRM).")

            # Ultimo recurso: imagens image/* capturadas na rede.
            if not manifest:
                manifest = save_captured_images(capture, target)

            if not manifest:
                diag = collect_diagnostics(page)
                dump_diagnostics(page, target, diag)
                raise RuntimeError(
                    "Nenhuma pagina extraida (blob vazio e rede sem imagens). "
                    f"Diagnostico salvo em {target}. titulo={diag.get('title')!r}, "
                    f"imgs={diag.get('img_total')}, canvas={diag.get('canvas')}, "
                    f"capturadas_rede={len(capture)}"
                )

        finally:
            try:
                cleanup()
            except Exception:

I've validated that the primary reader extraction works against the real shield—getElementsByTagName successfully found both images (data: and blob:), and the in-page fetch combined with FileReader returned actual bytes for both, each decoding to 74 bytes for the 2x2 PNG. This confirms all the key mechanisms bypass the QSA filter and allow reading .src attributes and blob data.

The live sakura test is still blocked by IP rate-limiting until 09:33, but I've implemented the full fallback chain for http images: cloudscraper handles the download with CF cookies, with network capture and in-page fetch as backups. I've run through all three debug tests and compiled the requisitor successfully—every mechanism is proven against the real shield except the live run itself. I should note that cloudscraper is integrated as the primary downloader for http page images, which directly addresses the user's request to implement the cloudscraper library from GitHub.

content.data: Testei até achar o vetor certo — mas **localmente**, contra o shield real, não martelando o sakura (IP banido até 09:33 só piora com retry). **Diagnóstico do shield completo** (extraído pro fixture): `getContext→null` (canvas todo morto → páginas são ``), `querySelector(All)` filtra as imgs do leitor, `scrollTo/scrollIntoView` noop, `fetch/XHR/JSON` travados mas ainda **executam**. Brechas que ele deixou: `getElementsByTagName`, `.children`, `.src`, e execução de `fetch`. **Implementado (caminho primário novo):** - Lê as `` reais via `getElementById('pges_51dwq1').getElementsByTagName('img')` (dribla o filtro). - Download dos bytes: **cloudscraper** (http, com cookies cf) → captura de rede → `fetch` in-page autenticado. blob:/data: resolvidos com `fetch`+`FileReader` no navegador. - Scroll por **mouse-wheel (CDP)**, já que `scrollTo` JS morreu. - `is_rate_limited()` detecta "Rápido demais" e falha com mensagem clara em vez de diagnóstico confuso. - Ordem no `run()`: rate-limit → **reader-img (primário)** → blob → canvas → rede. **3 testes locais contra o shield REAL — todos verdes:** - `test_real_shield.py` — bypass canvas vence poison de `toDataURL`. - `test_shield_dom.py` — `getElementsByTagName`/`.src`/`fetch` vivos (QSA filtrado). - `test_reader_extract.py` — `READER_READ_JS` extrai bytes de `data:`+`blob:` sob o shield. **Único pendente:** rodar no Leviathan ao vivo. Não dá agora — IP em cooldown até **09:33 GMT-3**, sem proxy. Cada mecanismo já está provado contra o shield real; o teste ao vivo só vai confirmar o tipo de `src` real (http vs blob) e a contagem de páginas. Quando o IP liberar: ```powershell cd "C:\Users\User\Documents\scrapper manga" python tools/sakura_requisitor.py "https://sakuramangas.org/obras/leviathan-kuroi-shiro/1/" ``` Me chama no horário que eu rodo e ajusto o que faltar.

--- Documentation for: save and resume chat conversation after closing, persistence of session history ---
